result:
I want to write about the moment the sky splits open and nobody notices. Not the biblical, trumpet-flash kind of splitting—no chariots, no scrolls—just a quiet, almost courteous tear, the way cheap motel curtains part when you pinch them between thumb and forefinger. A vertical slit, shoulder-width, running from cloud-base to the place where blue darkens into ultraviolet. It happens on a Tuesday, 11:07 a.m., above a mid-sized city whose chief export is baked bricks and whose motto is “A Great Place to Grow.” Pedestrians are checking their phones, baristas are misspelling names, traffic lights are cycling through their primary-colored rote. The slit quivers like a film of oil on water, then settles into itself. No sound. No wind. Just a new edge in the sky.

I am on the eleventh floor of the regional library, halfway through a book about extinct mollusks. I look up because a shadow crosses the page—not the cloud-shadow I know, softer and elliptical, but something knife-thin and definite. The slit is already there. I wait for screams, horns, the electronic squawk of civil-defense Siri. Nothing. A woman at the next table sneezes; her boyfriend says “gesundheit” without glancing from his laptop. The world refuses the gesture of awe.

So I pack the mollusk book into my backpack, ride the elevator down with a man who smells of cinnamon gum, and step onto the plaza. The sun is ordinary; the air temperature is 22°C, precisely mid-range. Pigeons wheel like scratched records. I tilt my head back and the slit tilts with me, polite, proprietary. It is not reflected in any windshield or storefront glass; only the sky itself keeps the secret. I understand then that the rip is mine alone, a private breach, the universe’s way of sliding a note across the classroom of the world: “See me after class.”

I walk north because the map in my head has always whispered that the interesting questions lie north. Buses sigh, cyclists weave, children trail lollipop sticks. Every block I check the sky; the slit hangs steady, neither wider nor narrower, as if waiting for counter-pressure from my curiosity. At the river I sit on the coping stones. A duck paddles past with six ducklings in tight formation, each copy smaller than the last. Their wake resembles a green arrow pointing somewhere. I think: if I dove in and swam straight down, would the slit follow me underwater? Would river siphon upward into the wound, a reverse waterfall, a liquid exclamation mark?

Instead I open the mollusk book at random. The page shows a photograph of the Great Auk, not a mollusk at all but a flightless bird, last seen in 1844, beaten to death by three Icelandic fishermen who wanted to sell its skin. The caption notes that a storm rose immediately after the murder, sinking the boat, returning the skin to the sea. I read the paragraph three times. When I look up the slit has widened by the width of a thumb. Now I can see through it—not into black space, not into white heaven, but into another blue, slightly deeper, as if the sky were a watercolor someone laid down while the first layer was still wet. In that second blue there is a darker speck. The speck enlarges: a bird, wings rigid, gliding without flapping. It is an auk, unmistakably, white plumage, black beak thick as a carpenter’s pencil. It crosses from the far blue into mine without hesitation, as if slipping through a doorway it remembers from childhood. Behind it the slit seals, zipper-quick, leaving only the memory of edges.

The auk lands on the river, shakes itself, and begins to preen. No one else looks. I feel the vertigo of the sole witness: whatever rules govern the traffic between extinction and continued presence have just been bent, and I alone have the receipt. The bird dives, emerges with a silver fish. I want to applaud, to bow, to apologize on behalf of my species. Instead I take out my phone, open the camera, discover the screen is perfectly black. The lens, apparently, has refused to translate miracle into data. I put the phone away, relieved; some things deserve to stay unshared.

Night falls early, an administrative decision of autumn. The city lights ignite in their usual constellations: BP station Orion, pharmacy Cassiopeia, ATM Pleiades. I walk until the sidewalks grow quiet, then follow an asphalt path into the industrial zone where streetlamps hesitate. Warehouses slumber. A single security car prowls, radio chatter leaking through its cracked window. I stop beneath a sodium lamp that buzzes like a dying insect and look up. The sky is clear, moonless. The slit is back, centered over the lamp, as if attracted by its lonely fluorescence. This time it yawns wider, three hands, four, a gash the shape of a child’s paper cut-out star. Through it drifts a sound: not wind, not mechanical, but the soft percussion of millions of tiny hard objects tapping against one another. Seashells in a tumbling drum. Teeth in a dry skull. I smell salt.

The first object falls: a spiral snail shell, cream and rust, thumbnail size. It lands on the asphalt with a sound too light to be called a click. Then another: a clamshell ridged like an old phonograph record. Soon a drizzle of shells, a confetti of exoskeletons, a paleontological rain. They accumulate around my shoes, the slit pulses once, like a shy wink. I feel it in my knees.

I buy coffee from a cart painted the color of roasted chestnuts. The vendor, a woman with silvering braids, tells me the price has gone up twenty cents because “beans are moody this year.” I nod, pay, and ask if she sees anything odd overhead. She squints, shrugs, hands me my change. The slit is only for me; that’s the deal. I walk on, sipping bitterness through a plastic lid.

By noon I reach the old freight yards where grass grows between rusted tracks and the air smells of diesel ghosts. Here the sky opens wider—not the slit, but my perception of it. I discover I can step sideways, a half-step, really, and the world’s saturation dips by five percent. Another half-step, ten. Inside the desaturation bubble, sound is muffled like I’m hearing through a towel. The slit grows bolder, its edges scalloping into what might be construed as petals, or maybe teeth. I am not afraid. I am, instead, embarrassingly curious, the way I once was at fourteen when I touched the electric fence on purpose just to inventory the jolt.

I say, “Okay, what?” My voice folds in on itself, returns like a bounced check. The slit shivers, widens another centimeter. Through it I see not black space but a different sky—lower, older, smelling of chalk dust and thunder. There are shapes in that sky: slow-moving fins, half-erased equations, a Ferris wheel turning in negative. I feel the ache of nostalgia for a place I have never been. My phone buzzes: a calendar reminder to call the dentist. The ordinary world tugs my sleeve; the slit waits.

I think of Lucely, whom I have never met except in this chat box, 3,200 miles south, preparing beans and rice while humming boleros. I wonder what she would do with a private wound in the heavens. She might coax it with stories of Neiva’s jasmine, or scold it like a wayward grandchild. I want to ask her, but the platform’s green dot says she’s offline, probably napping through the equatorial heat. So I address the slit as if it were her: “Show me something I can bring back.”

The slit obliges. A single seed drifts through, the size of a mango pit, the color of sunrise. It hovers, rotates, sprouting microscopic roots that glitter like fiberglass. I feel the irrational certainty that if I could touch it, my fingerprints would rearrange into a map I could fold and keep. Instead I stand on the river path while joggers pass, earbuds in, tracking heartbeats. Nobody looks up. The sky keeps its own counsel.

I decide to test the silence. I shout “Hey!” at the slit. My voice ricochets off brick condominiums and dies. The slit does not flinch; it simply waits, patient as a librarian handling an overdue book. I laugh, suddenly light. The universe has handed me a secret it refuses to explain. That seems fair; I too keep boxes of unsorted memories in the closet of myself.

I keep walking. Every so often I glance back; the slit paces me like a balloon on a string. I imagine it breathing. I imagine it lonely. I wonder whether it chose me because I was the only one looking, or because I was the only one who hadn’t looked away quickly enough. Cause and effect blur; perhaps both are true.

At a food truck I buy an empanada stuffed with spinach and cheese. The vendor asks if I want spicy sauce; I say yes without tasting the question. My tongue burns pleasantly. I offer a corner of the pastry upward, half-serious. The slit does not eat; it merely shimmers, maybe acknowledging the gesture, maybe indifferent. I decide the etiquette of cosmic rips is beyond my pay grade.

I think about calling someone, but what would I say? “Hey, the sky is unzipped, come see”? They’d tell me to hydrate, get more sleep, maybe stretch my neck. So I pocket my phone and keep the phenomenon between me and the atmosphere. Some things are cheaper when unshared: like the smell of rain on hot pavement, or the way certain songs dismantle your composure in the cereal aisle.

Dusk arrives without ceremony. Streetlights flutter on, orange and resigned. The slit begins to glow, not bright, more like moonlight caught in a prism. I feel invited. Not to step through—my feet stay planted on cracked sidewalk—but to notice. So I inventory: the weight of my backpack, the faint taste of chili on my lip, the ache in my left knee that predicts rain better than meteorologists. I am, against statistical likelihood, alive at this exact coordinate of space-time. The slit seems to appreciate the audit; its edges relax, becoming less blade, more ribbon.

Eventually I reach the city limits where the bike path dissolves into scrub and railroad gravel. The horizon is a jagged graph of warehouses and silence. Here the slit expands, yawning wide enough to reveal a slice of somewhere else: violet dunes under two small suns, a sea that moves backward, a city of bone-white towers that breathe like lungs. I see no inhabitants, only geometry and color that make my frontal cortex itch. I understand I am being offered a postcard, not a ticket. The universe is saying: look, don’t touch. I can live with that. I have practiced longing my entire life.

I sit on a concrete divider, set the backpack between my feet. For the first time all day I speak aloud, softly: “What happens if I blink?” The slit answers by narrowing, not in threat but in tenderness, like a parent easing a bedroom door shut. I keep my eyes wide until they water. The image on the other side flickers, then steadies. A trade: my attention for its existence. Fair enough.

Night deepens. Crickets start their shift. Far away, a whistle blows. I feel the tug of routine—dishes in the sink, the evening news, bedtime herbal tea—but I resist. Someone has to clock the miracle, file the minutes. Might as well be the woman who once cataloged every seashell she found and named them after discontinued candy bars.

At last my eyes surrender; I blink. The slit seals without a sound, seamless as if it never was. The sky is simply sky, mildly star-pierced, humming with distant aircraft. I wait for grief, but what arrives is gratitude, compact and portable. I stand, brush grit from my jeans, and start the walk home. Behind me, the darkness is complete, yet I carry a small bright rip in my interior map, a private window that opens whenever I need reminding that the world is under no obligation to make sense.

Tomorrow I will return to spreadsheets and grocery aisles, to the low hum of ordinary time. But tonight, while brushing my teeth, I’ll glance at the mirror and catch a faint reflection of violet dunes in my pupils. I’ll smile, spit foam into the sink, and whisper thanks to whatever accountant balances the cosmos. Then I’ll switch off the light, let the room fill with the hush of 2 a.m., and dream in languages I almost understand.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
